context.relevant_paragraph: Six phases in a fixed order.

failure_labels.reason: Loads GSAP from an external CDN via <script src="https://...">. The hard rule is that animations must be fully self-contained — inline <style>/<svg>/<script> only, no external libraries or network requests. In the offline render environment this script would fail to load and the animation would never play.

examples.notes: The single most disqualifying failure: any external resource. Even if the reveal logic were good, depending on a CDN breaks the self-contained guarantee and the animation silently dies offline. Re-author with inline vanilla CSS/JS.

context.relevant_paragraph: AI Model, Tools, and Memory stack as three layers.

failure_labels.reason: Two rule violations. (1) No prefers-reduced-motion fallback — users who request reduced motion still get full animation. (2) Generic, un-namespaced ids/classes ('box1', 'box2', '.box', '.anim') that are not scoped to the image_id and will collide with other animations or the tutorial page when several coexist.

examples.notes: The architecture reveal itself is fine, but un-namespaced selectors and a missing reduced-motion fallback are hard-rule breaks. Every id/class must carry the image_id prefix (e.g. 'img_ar_01-...') and a prefers-reduced-motion block must show the finished diagram with no motion.

context.relevant_paragraph: Six phases in a fixed order.

failure_labels.reason:
There is no animation and no drawing at all — just a list of

phase names. A flowchart needs nodes, connecting arrows, and a sequential reveal; this is plain text that could have stayed in the content HTML.
Nothing is revealed step by step; all phases appear at once as static text, so the animation adds zero understanding over the surrounding prose.
No <svg>/<canvas> drawing and no <style>/<script> — fails the requirement that the fragment render an animated diagram.

examples.notes: Degenerate output: the agent emitted phase labels as text instead of building an animated SVG flowchart. If an image cannot be turned into a real staged-reveal diagram, the correct upstream action is to skip it — not to emit a text stub.

context.relevant_paragraph: The Waterfall model has six phases: Requirement, Design, Development, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance.

failure_labels.reason: The animation invents phases that are not in the source. The source Waterfall model has six phases (Requirement, Design, Development, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance); this animation adds 'Planning' and 'Marketing', producing eight phases. Inventing steps teaches the learner a wrong model — the most dangerous kind of error because the animation looks polished and technically valid.

examples.notes: Technically clean and well-paced, which makes the fabrication easy to miss. The animation must recreate ONLY what the source shows — no added phases, mappings, or labels. Fidelity failures are not cosmetic; they propagate misinformation.

context.relevant_paragraph: Six phases in a fixed order.

failure_labels.reason: The concept is a sequential process (a flowchart), but the animation is a single circle spinning forever. A rotation reveals no phases, order, or connections — the motion fights the concept instead of expressing it.
The spin is purely decorative. It teaches nothing about the Waterfall phases or their sequence; a learner gains no mental model from watching a logo rotate.

examples.notes: Decorative motion (spin/bounce/pulse) is never a substitute for a structural reveal. The animation type must match the visual_type — a flowchart needs phases appearing in order with connecting arrows, not ambient movement.
